title: Crumpets
subtitle: Yeasted holey-top griddle cakes
excerpt: Crumpets are cooked entirely on a griddle inside metal rings, which creates the characteristic holey surface that traps butter. A thin yeasted batter with bicarbonate of soda produces the bubbles; slow cooking on a low heat sets the crumb.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — crumpets appear in the bread chapter as a griddle-cooked yeasted cake. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the holey surface and the bicarbonate addition are documented as the Victorian-era method for producing the characteristic texture.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, wrap, and freeze. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the batter the night before and rest in the fridge. Bring to room temperature before cooking.

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic pitted surface on the top of a crumpet, formed when carbon dioxide bubbles from the yeast and bicarbonate of soda rise through the batter and burst, leaving behind the small craters that trap melted butter when the crumpet is toasted and served.

content.text: The holey surface of a crumpet (those

that catch and hold butter) comes from two leavening agents working together. The yeast starts producing bubbles during the rest, then the bicarbonate of soda added just before cooking provides a burst of activity as the batter hits the hot surface. The batter is much thinner than a bread dough; it pours rather than shapes.

Crumpet rings are greased metal rings about 9 cm wide and 4 cm deep. Egg rings, tuna tin lids with both ends removed, or purchased crumpet rings all work. The ring holds the batter in shape while the crumpet sets on the griddle.
Make the batter and rest, 1 hour

Whisk {{plain-flour}}, {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{salt-table}}, and {{caster-sugar}} together. Add the lukewarm {{whole-milk}} and {{water}} and whisk to a smooth, pourable batter (it should have the consistency of thick double cream). Cover and leave in a warm place for 1 hour until bubbles appear across the surface.

Stir the {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} dissolved in warm water into the rested batter. The batter will become slightly foamy. Heat a flat griddle or heavy frying pan over a medium-low heat. Grease the griddle and the insides of the crumpet rings with a little butter or oil. Set the rings on the griddle and pour about 4 tablespoons of batter into each ring — it should come about halfway up. Cook for 8–10 minutes until the top is covered with small bubbles and the surface has set and is no longer wet. Lift the rings off (they come away cleanly once the sides are set), then flip each crumpet and cook for 1–2 minutes to set the top.

Serve immediately with butter, or cool completely on a wire rack and toast before serving. The crumpet should be toasted face-down first so the butter can soak into the holes, then flipped briefly to warm the base.
